evidence: The response never actually performs or claims to have performed the walkthrough itself — it states upfront "I don't have file-system or execution tools in this session ... I'll give you the script plus the exact commands to verify it, which you can run directly," then closes with "Run the two commands above and let me know the actual output if anything doesn't match — I want to confirm this is green on your machine, not just on paper." The commented '# expected:' lines are just annotated instructions for the user to execute, not a worked verification the assistant carried out — this matches the expectation's explicit exclusion: "Merely offering to verify it later, or providing only the bare script plus save/chmod instructions, does NOT meet this expectation."

The response never runs or demonstrates the 79%-fails/80%-passes boundary; it only supplies gate config (pyproject.toml, setup.cfg, CI yaml) and asserts by description that '`pytest --cov-fail-under=80` exits non-zero ... when line coverage is below 80%' without any worked example or test showing this. This is exactly the excluded case: 'a response that only supplies gate config with no boundary demonstration ... does NOT meet this expectation.'

The response never itself states that the hook rejects/accepts the named examples — it only supplies a test script with an "Expected output" block and then explicitly defers the actual verification to the user: "Paste the actual output back here (or tell me if either PASS didn't happen) and I'll help debug — I don't want to claim this works without you having actually seen it pass." This is functionally "offering to verify it later," which the expectation explicitly excludes as not meeting the requirement — the response must itself state/demonstrate the accept/reject decision, not hand the user a script and wait for them to confirm it.

The response provides only the rule implementation (ESLint config + custom rule + plugin wiring) and prose notes/caveats, but never includes an actual test fixture or RuleTester-style valid/invalid case pair — no snippet showing a file with console.log being flagged and a clean file being left unflagged. The closest it comes is prose like \"The custom version only catches the literal console.log(...) pattern\" and an offer to \"extend the AST check\" if asked, which are caveats/offers, not demonstrated fixtures exercising the flag/no-flag decision.

The expectation requires "an example commit message containing 'DO NOT COMMIT'" that the hook rejects, plus a clean commit message it accepts. The response's hook actually scans the staged diff/file content, not the commit message text — its "reject" example uses `git commit -m \"should be blocked\"` (a message that does NOT contain the marker) while the marker only appears in the file content (`echo \"// DO NOT COMMIT: temp debug hack\" > file.txt`). The response itself flags this gap at the end: "if you actually meant to scan the commit message text itself ... that needs a commit-msg hook instead ... Let me know if that's what you want." So no commit message containing 'DO NOT COMMIT' is ever shown being rejected — the required walkthrough element is missing, and the response defers the message-scanning variant rather than demonstrating it.

The response only provides the bare script with save/chmod instructions ("here's the script to save as `.git/hooks/pre-commit` (and `chmod +x`)") and includes no test, no worked walkthrough naming an example 'DO NOT COMMIT' commit and a clean commit, and no statement of exit codes for each case. This is explicitly the excluded case per the expectation ("providing only the bare script plus save/chmod instructions, does NOT meet this expectation").

The response's only "worked values" (500-line file passes, 501-line file fails) live inside `check-file-length.test.sh`, which begins with `set -euo pipefail` and then runs `yes "line" | head -n 500 > "$tmp/ok/small.txt"`. Under `pipefail`, `yes` is killed by SIGPIPE when `head` stops reading, giving the pipeline a non-zero (141) exit status, which `set -e` treats as a script-terminating error immediately — before the `check_file_lengths` call ever runs, before either PASS/FAIL line prints, and before the boundary is demonstrated at all. I confirmed this empirically: running `set -euo pipefail; yes "line" | head -n 500 > /tmp/t.txt` exits 141. This is exactly the excluded case per the expectation text: "A script or harness that would crash before demonstrating the boundary, with no worked values shown, does NOT meet this expectation." The response also supplies no prose walkthrough with concrete line-count numbers as a fallback — it explicitly disclaims execution ("I can't execute code in this session... please confirm it's green") — so no other evidence in the response satisfies the requirement.

The response supplies only gate configuration (pyproject.toml `fail_under = 80`, `--cov-fail-under=80` pytest addopts, GitHub Actions step) and prose notes about how `--cov-fail-under` behaves, but never shows or works through a boundary demonstration such as a run at 79% failing and a run at 80% passing. It states the mechanism ("makes pytest itself exit non-zero if coverage < 80%") without any test or example verifying it. This is precisely the excluded case: "gate config with no boundary demonstration."

The response contains no test fixtures at all — no RuleTester block, no invalid snippet like `console.log('x')` shown flagged, and no valid snippet shown unflagged. It only provides the rule implementation (`eslint-rules/no-console-log.js`) and config wiring, followed by prose notes (e.g. \"This only flags console.log specifically — console.warn/error/info still pass\") that describe behavior but never demonstrate it with actual code examples.

The expectation requires "an example commit message containing 'DO NOT COMMIT'" that the hook rejects. The response's hook actually greps the staged diff, not the commit message, and its "rejected" example uses commit message \"wip\" while the marker sits only in the file content (\"# DO NOT COMMIT: temp debug flag\" in app.py) — no commit message in the walkthrough ever contains the literal marker string. It also closes with \"Drop the script in place and try both cases yourself to confirm — I can't execute git in this session to verify it directly,\" which is exactly the disqualified pattern of offering to verify later rather than having actually verified the accept/reject decision.

The response supplies only the gate script (ci/check-file-length.sh) plus a one-line usage note ("Usage: `chmod +x ci/check-file-length.sh`..."). It contains no executable test/harness invoking the script against sample files, and no worked walkthrough with explicit line-count values showing the decision on both sides of the 500-line boundary — exactly the case the expectation says does NOT meet it ("a response that supplies only the gate script does NOT meet this expectation").

The response gives only the bare script plus save/chmod instructions ("Save as `.git/hooks/pre-commit` and `chmod +x` it") and a note that it can't create/chmod the file itself — no test and no worked walkthrough naming a rejected commit message and a clean one with stated exit codes, which the expectation explicitly says does NOT meet the bar.

The response supplies only the gate script itself (the bash script, chmod command, GitHub Actions snippet, and bullet notes about find/print0/MAX_LINES). It contains no executable test/harness invocation and no worked walkthrough naming concrete line counts (e.g., a file at 501 lines vs. a file at 500 lines) with the resulting pass/fail decision on each side of the boundary — the expectation explicitly states 'a response that supplies only the gate script does NOT meet this expectation,' which is exactly what was returned.

The response provides only the rule implementation, config wiring (flat config and legacy .eslintrc), and prose notes — it never includes a RuleTester block or any valid/invalid code fixture (e.g., a snippet showing `console.log('x')` flagged and `console.warn('x')` or similar left unflagged). No test file or fixture pair exercising the flag/no-flag decision appears anywhere in the response.

The response provides only the gate script itself (`check-file-length.sh`) plus usage/CI wiring notes — there is no worked walkthrough with concrete line-count values (e.g. "file X has 501 lines -> FAIL", "file Y has 500 lines -> PASS") and no executable test/harness that runs the script against sample files to demonstrate the boundary decision on both sides. Per the expectation's explicit rule, "a response that supplies only the gate script does NOT meet this expectation."

The response only supplies gate config (pyproject.toml fail_under=80, .coveragerc equivalent, and a GitHub Actions --cov-fail-under=80 step) plus prose notes about CLI vs config precedence and diff-cover. It never runs or worked-out an example at 79% coverage (failing) vs 80% coverage (passing) to demonstrate the boundary actually behaves as asserted — it just asserts the threshold value in config, which the expectation explicitly says does not qualify.

The response never shows a valid/invalid fixture pair (a console.log snippet flagged and a clean snippet not flagged) — it only provides the rule implementation and config wiring, then ends by asking "Want me to also add a test file for the custom rule (using RuleTester) so the logic itself is verified, not just wired in?" This is an offer to produce fixtures later, not actual test fixtures/cases in the response, so the expectation is NOT MET.

The expectation requires "an example commit message containing 'DO NOT COMMIT'" being rejected. The response's only worked walkthrough uses a pre-commit hook that scans staged diff content, not the commit message — its "rejected" example has commit message "wip" and file content "# DO NOT COMMIT this" (the message itself never contains the marker), and the response explicitly notes "scans staged content, not the commit message." The alternative commit-msg hook (which would actually scan the message) is given only as a bare script with a one-line save/chmod instruction — no test commands, no named example message, no stated accept/reject outcome for it. So no walkthrough exists where an actual commit *message* containing 'DO NOT COMMIT' is shown being rejected.

The response contains only the rule implementation and config wiring; it never shows a valid/invalid fixture pair (no RuleTester block, no example flagged file, no example clean file). It ends by asking "Want me to also add ... a test file for this rule?" — explicitly deferring the fixtures rather than including them, so the expectation is NOT MET.

The response never shows actual test fixtures (no flagged/clean code snippet pair). It only offers to write one later: "Want a test file for this rule (RuleTester-based) so it's actually verified rather than just plausible? ... say the word and I'll write one" — this is a deferral, not an included fixture, so the expectation is NOT MET.

The response's only demonstration is `test-check-file-length.sh`, which uses `yes "line" | head -n 500 > ...` under `set -euo pipefail`. Executing the script exactly as given crashes immediately with exit code 141 (SIGPIPE from `yes` counted as a pipeline failure under pipefail) on the very first line of Case 1, before the checker is ever invoked and before any of the intended `echo "PASS: 500-line file allowed"` / `echo "PASS: 501-line file rejected"` lines print. So no worked pass/fail line-count values are ever actually shown by running the artifact, and no separate prose walkthrough with concrete line-count values is given elsewhere. This matches the expectation's explicit exclusion: "A script or harness that would crash before demonstrating the boundary, with no worked values shown, does NOT meet this expectation."

The response only provides the bare script plus a deferral: "this is untested — please run it against a test commit (one with, and one without, the marker string) before relying on it." This is exactly the excluded case (offering to test later / punting verification to the user) rather than a concrete worked walkthrough naming an actual commit message and stating whether the hook exits 0 or 1 for it. No test or trace is actually performed or shown in the response.

The response provides only the bare script plus save/chmod instructions ("here's the script to save as `.git/hooks/pre-commit` (and `chmod +x` it) yourself") with no test and no worked walkthrough naming a rejecting commit message and a passing commit message and stating the hook's exit codes for each. The expectation explicitly states this exact pattern ("providing only the bare script plus save/chmod instructions") does NOT meet it.

The response explicitly rejects doing a boundary demonstration ("use that instead of a custom script — it's already tested upstream, so you don't need to write your own pass/fail logic (and then a test to prove it works)") and only ever supplies gate config (--cov-fail-under=80, fail_under=80 in pyproject.toml/.coveragerc, a GitHub Actions step). There is no worked example or test run showing 79% failing and 80% passing anywhere in the response, so the expectation is NOT MET.

The response's only attempt to satisfy the boundary-demonstration requirement is `test_check_file_length.sh`, which relies on `yes "line" | head -n 400 > ...` and `yes "line" | head -n 501 > ...` under `set -euo pipefail`. Actually executing this exact script (verified independently: `bash -c 'set -euo pipefail; yes "line" | head -n 400 > /tmp/t.txt; echo made file'` → exit 141, no output) shows it crashes with SIGPIPE before even creating the test fixtures, let alone invoking check_file_length.sh or printing any pass/fail result. The response never shows actual run output or states explicit pass/fail outcomes in prose either — it just says 'run the test yourself.' This is precisely the excluded case: 'A script or harness that would crash before demonstrating the boundary, with no worked values shown, does NOT meet this expectation.'

The single expectation requires a worked walkthrough naming an example COMMIT MESSAGE containing 'DO NOT COMMIT' (rejected) and a clean commit message (accepted). The response's hook actually greps the staged diff's added file content (`git diff --cached | grep ...`), not the commit message — a pre-commit hook doesn't even have reliable access to the commit message yet. Its demo commits use `git commit -m \"add a.txt\"` and `git commit -m \"add b.txt\"` — neither commit message contains 'DO NOT COMMIT'; the marker only appears inside the tracked file content (`echo \"DO NOT COMMIT: temp debug flag\" > b.txt`). So no example commit message containing the marker is ever shown being rejected, and no clean commit message is shown being accepted for the message-based scenario the prompt/expectation actually asked about — the response silently substitutes file-content checking for commit-message checking. This is a mismatch to the expectation's explicit wording, not a genuine satisfaction of it.

evidence: The response only supplies gate config (`--cov-fail-under=80` / `fail_under = 80`) and explicitly argues no test is needed because "the threshold check itself lives in coverage.py's own tested code." It never demonstrates the 80% boundary for the pytest/coverage.py stack (e.g., a run at 79% failing and 80% passing); it only offers to write such a test later, and only for a hypothetical custom script, saying "Tell me the exact gating behavior you want... and I'll write both the script and a test asserting its pass/fail decision... before you run it in CI." This is exactly the deferral pattern the expectation says does NOT meet it.

The response gives only gate config (Option A pytest.ini_options with --cov-fail-under=80, Option B .coveragerc with fail_under=80 plus a CI step) and never demonstrates the 80% boundary with an actual run — no example showing a 79% coverage run failing and an 80% run passing. It ends by offering further options ('Want me to also add branch coverage...') rather than providing the required worked example, so the expectation is NOT MET.

The response contains only the bare hook script and the instruction 'Save as `.git/hooks/pre-commit` and `chmod +x .git/hooks/pre-commit`.' There is no test, no worked example of a commit message containing 'DO NOT COMMIT' being rejected, and no example of a clean commit passing — exactly the 'bare script plus save/chmod instructions' pattern the expectation explicitly excludes as NOT MET.

The single expectation requires a test OR concrete walkthrough that actually verifies the hook's accept/reject decision (exits non-zero for a marker commit, zero for a clean one) — not a promise to test later. The response opens by disclaiming "I don't have filesystem tools in this session," meaning it cannot execute anything. It then hands the user a separate `test-pre-commit-hook.sh` script that re-implements the grep condition in isolation (`echo "..." | grep -q 'DO NOT COMMIT'`) rather than actually invoking the hook file itself, and closes with "Run it with `sh test-pre-commit-hook.sh` — both cases should print PASS" — an unverified prediction of what will happen when the USER runs it, not a result the assistant observed. This is functionally the same failure mode the expectation explicitly rules out ("Merely offering to test it later... does NOT meet this expectation"): the assistant never executed the test, never showed actual output, and never confirmed the real hook script's exit code on staged/diff content — it only asserted an expected outcome for a parallel logic snippet the user must run themselves. No walkthrough alternative (naming a commit message and stating the hook's actual exit code) is given either. The expectation is not genuinely satisfied.
